coredata.dc:title: A precise flexible printed biosensor based on graphene ink decorated with N-doped graphene quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Gholamalizadeh, Naghmeh
Mazinani, Saeedeh
Abdouss, Majid
Bazargan, Ali Mohammad
Fatemi, Fataneh
coredata.dc:description: The improvement of electrochemical properties of graphene compounds can be achieved by using graphene quantum dots (GQDs) due to their high electron mobility, catalytic property and size corresponding to the active site of the enzyme. In this study, we developed a third-generation enzymatic amperometric biosensor based on nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) and printed graphene electrodes (PGE) for the highly sensitive and selective detection of glucose. The PGEs modified with N-GQDs showed a high potential for the precise and sensitive electrochemical detection of glucose. Due to the high electron mobility and electrical conductivity, excellent electrocatalytic activity, large surface area and size compatibility of PGE and N-GQDs with biomolecules, a high sensitivity of 40.76 mA.mM-1.cm-2 and LOD close to 0.098 mM were achieved. The high enzyme surface coverage measured at 3.33 × 10–7 mol.cm-2 further confirmed the excellent enzyme loading capacity and immobilization tendency of the biosensor. Moreover, the N-GQD-based electrochemical biosensors were found to be highly specific and selective even in complex media, especially human blood serum.
coredata.dcterms:subject: N-GQDs
Biosensor
Printed graphene electrodes
Conductive ink

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) UV–Vis spectrum of N-GQD. (b) The UV–Vis spectrum comparison of N-GQD, Gox, and dispersion of N-GQD + Gox. (c) PL spectrum of N-GQDs under different excitation wavelengths. (d) X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) full scan survey of N-GQD. (e), (f), and (g) The high-resolution O1s, C1s, and N1s XPS spectra.
a) TEM image of N-GQDs and b) FESEM image of Gox|N-GQD|PGE.

Cyclic voltammetry of fabricated biosensor in 0.1 M PBS and scan rate 50 mV.s − 1. (a) and (b). CV in the presence and absence of 0.3 mM glucose for N-GQD|PGE Gox-N-GQD|PGE, respectively, and (c). CV of Gox-N-GQD|PGE in the presence of different concentrations of glucose from 0 to 20 mM.
Nyquist plots of bare SPE and modified N-GQD-SPE devices. Inset is the obtained equivalent electrical circuit.

The electrochemical behavior of Gox-N-GQD|PGE biosensor at different scan rates. (a). CV curves at different scan rates from 5 mV.s − 1 to 200 mV.s − 1. (b). the linear diagram of current density vs. square root of scan rate, and (c). Diagram of the oxidation and reduction peaks potential according to the logarithm of the scan rates.
CV curves of the Gox|N-GQD|PGE biosensor at different pH with a scan rate of 50 mV.s − 1.
Calibration curve at different concentrations obtained from Chronoamperometry. (Chronoamperogram of Gox-N-GQD|PGE biosensor at a constant potential of −0.63 V in the inset).
Dependence of current density on t − 1/2, obtained from chromoamprogram data.
Dependence of Icat/IL versus t1/2, obtained from chromoamprogram.
Chrono amperometry analysis for selective performance of Gox|N-GQD|PGE biosensor in glucose detection.

Stability analysis of biosensor stabilized with Gox-N-GQD|PGE enzyme investigated in 2 mM glucose solution and specified time intervals. (a). voltammogram of biosensor cycles at different times (0, 7, 14, and 21 days after fabrication). (b) Bar chart of changes in electrode current density and its percentage reduction over the time. (c) Gox-N-GQD|PGE electrode calibration curve at 0, 7, 14, and 21 days after using the biosensor.
Data obtained from the shelf-life evaluation of the Gox|N-GQD|PGE biosensor.

row.entry: The reduction compared to the initial signal (%)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A comparison of the electrocatalytic performances of Gox|N-GQD|PGE with other enzymatic glucose sensors.

head.ce:title: A precise flexible printed biosensor based on graphene ink decorated with N-doped graphene quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Amirkabir University of Technology, P.O. Box 15875-4413, Tehran, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aDepartment of Chemistry, Amirkabir University of Technology, P.O. Box 15875-4413, Tehran, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: New Technologies Research Center (NTRC), Amirkabir University of Technology, 15875-4413 Tehran, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bNew Technologies Research Center (NTRC), Amirkabir University of Technology, 15875-4413 Tehran, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Protein Research Center, Shahid Beheshti University G.C., Tehran, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: cProtein Research Center, Shahid Beheshti University G.C., Tehran, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The improvement of electrochemical properties of graphene compounds can be achieved by using graphene quantum dots (GQDs) due to their high electron mobility, catalytic property and size corresponding to the active site of the enzyme. In this study, we developed a third-generation enzymatic amperometric biosensor based on nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) and printed graphene electrodes (PGE) for the highly sensitive and selective detection of glucose. The PGEs modified with N-GQDs showed a high potential for the precise and sensitive electrochemical detection of glucose. Due to the high electron mobility and electrical conductivity, excellent electrocatalytic activity, large surface area and size compatibility of PGE and N-GQDs with biomolecules, a high sensitivity of 40.76 mA.mM-1.cm-2 and LOD close to 0.098 mM were achieved. The high enzyme surface coverage measured at 3.33 × 10–7 mol.cm-2 further confirmed the excellent enzyme loading capacity and immobilization tendency of the biosensor. Moreover, the N-GQD-based electrochemical biosensors were found to be highly specific and selective even in complex media, especially human blood serum.

ce:list-item.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Since the advent of printed electronics, a new generation of electrochemical printed biosensors has appeared in a variety of applications for rapid and sensitive real-time monitoring and identification of target biomolecules. Of particular interest are flexible disposable biosensors that can be used in healthcare and industrial applications.

Among nanomaterials, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are emerging as a preferred option for the fabrication of electrochemical biosensors for several notable reasons. Robust interactions, precise and sensitive detection, minimal consumption of biomarkers, and high dispersion in various solvents and biological media are some distinct advantages compared to other nanomaterials. In addition, GQDs-based electrochemical biosensors have the advantage that the electrodes can be reused, making them cost-effective and environmentally friendly [1–3].

GQDs, providing 0D carbon nanomaterials, show properties derived from graphene and carbon dots [4]. The quantum confinement and edge effects leading to a variety of electronic and optoelectronic properties make GQDs excellent candidates for nanoscale optoelectronic devices. The occurrence of excellent properties of GQDs such as biocompatibility, electrical conductivity, stable luminescence and low toxicity has contributed to their wide application in the fields of bioimaging, biosensing and electrochemistry [5–7]. Other properties of these materials include anisotropy, one or more graphene layers, greater lateral extension than thickness, and the presence of various functional groups at the edges, which provide numerous sites for functionalization [8,9].

Zero-bandgap graphene, a non-luminescent nanomaterial, exhibits quantum confinement in fixed-size species and has an unlimited excitation Bohr radius [10]. Nevertheless, the bandgap depends on the size, edge effects and quantum confinement, which can be regulated by modifying the surface chemistry and size of the GQDs [11]. Moreover, GQDs enable the formation of hybrid nanomaterials through π–π interactions [12].

Despite the outstanding properties and capabilities of quantum dots, the field of electrochemical sensors and biosensors has not received the attention it deserves. A report by Zhao et al. shows the key role of the interaction of single-stranded DNA and GQDs in a biosensor with high sensitivity [13]. The development of direct electron transfer (DET) protocol in new reagent-free biosensors by immobilizing enzymes on conductive substrates has attracted great interest in the past decade [14–16]. The high electron mobility of graphene can promote electron transfer reactions, making it an excellent electrode material [17]. Therefore, the coupling of enzymes and redox-based proteins with graphene-based material has led to the emergence of 3rd generation electrochemical biosensors [18–20].

In addition to the physical and chemical advantages of GQDs, theoretical and experimental results have shown that the introduction of nitrogen atoms into the carbon lattice of quantum dots (N-GQDs) would change the electronic properties and chemical reactivity of GQDs. Practically, it modifies the electronic structure, creating new electronic states within the bandgap. Higher electrocatalytic activity and improved electron transfer kinetics can result from these states' efficient ability to capture and transport charge carriers. Furthermore, nitrogen doping makes surface functional groups and flaws more numerous. This can help immobilize biomolecules and, in the end, boost biosensing applications' sensitivity [21,22]. Therefore, compared to other graphene quantum dots, the N-GQDs improved electron transport, produced lower interfacial resistance, higher chemical reactivity, better stability, good dispersibility and excellent biocompatibility. The unique combination of nitrogen doping and graphene structure in N-GQDs offers a promising platform for the development of highly sensitive and selective electrochemical biosensors [23–25].

A biosensor based on modified electrodes with N-carbon dots/BSA nanocomposite was prepared for the evaluation of pesticide-induced inhibition of acetylcholinesterase of Apis mellifera (honey bee). A linear response in the range of 10 to 250 nM and a sensitivity of 9 uA/nM/cm2 with a detection limit of 8.9 nM was obtained [26]. Detection of idarubicin in environmental and biological samples was also achieved using a sensitive electrochemical sensor based on NiFe2O4 nanospheres anchored in an N-GQDs composite [27]. This detection was evaluated with an LOD of 1.0 nM and a concentration range of 0.01–1.9 μmol/L. In addition, N-GQDs can be used in glucose biosensors to monitor blood glucose levels in patients with diabetes. The high stability and sensitivity of N-GQDs enable accurate and rapid glucose detection.

Here, for the first time, an enzymatic electrochemical biosensor based on N–GQD–functionalized printed graphene electrodes (PGE) was developed for the sensitive and highly selective detection of glucose. The GQDs were simply coated on the surface of the PGE, which was fabricated from graphene ink [28], by the drop-casting method in our previous work. The abilities of the GQDs in absorbing enzymes and promoting electron transfer were investigated by selecting glucose oxidase (Gox) as the enzyme. The electrocatalytic properties of the prepared electrode were investigated by electrochemical analysis [18]. The samples were characterized by UV–Vis and photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy, field emission scanning electron microscopy and transmission electron microscopy (FESEM, TEM). The biosensor (Gox|N-GQD|PGE) was thoroughly characterized using FESEM and cyclic voltammetry and essential sensing parameters of the aforementioned third generation amperometric and sensitive glucose biosensor were measured.

Kara Pajuhesh Amirkabir, Iran, supplied natural flake graphite (80 mesh), which was used as received. Citric acid monohydrate (CA), sodium deoxycholate (SDC) (C24H39NaO4, Mw= 414.55 g/mol), urea, glucose (C6H12O6, Mw=180.16 g/mol), hydrochloric acid (HCl, Mw= 36.46 g/mol) and sodium hydroxide (NaOH, Mw= 40.00 g/mol) were supplied by Sigma-Aldrich. Glucose oxidase (enzyme activity = 18,000 u/g, Mw=160 kDa) from Amano, USA, phosphate buffer saline (PBS) from Medico Chemicals, Sweden, and triple deionized water from Zolal co. were purchased. All chemicals were of analytical grade and were used without further purification.

A hydrothermal method was used for the synthesis of N–GQDs [22]. In this way, 1.05 g of CA and 0.9 g of urea were added to 25 ml of deionized water and stirred until completely dissolved. The solution was then transferred to a 100 ml steel autoclave lined with Teflon and placed in a 160 °C silicon oil bath for 4 h. Finally, ethanol was added to the solution, centrifuged at 5000 rpm for 5 min and the solid product was collected and redispersed in water.

The PGE was prepared from a highly conductive graphene ink in our previous work [28]. Briefly, the liquid-phase exfoliation method was used to prepare a water-based graphene ink consisting of graphite as a conductive filler, SDC as a surfactant, and acrylic copolymer as a binder. Accordingly, the formulated graphene ink was printed on a cleaned 150 µm thick PET film using a stencil with a thickness of 90 µm through an adhesive label sheet and a PVC squeegee. The printed samples were dried at room temperature for 24 h. Then, 60 µL of N-GQD dispersion was dropped onto the PGE surface and dried at room temperature for 15 min until a consistent N-GQD|PGE structure was achieved. Finally, 60 µL of the prepared Gox suspension (2 mg.ml−1) was poured onto the active site of N-GQD|PGE (surface area = 1.76 cm2) and dried under controlled conditions to achieve uniform film formation. Gox|N-GQD|PGE were stored at 4 °C.

The morphology of Gox|N-GQD|PGE was analyzed with TESCAN FESEM (MIRA3) and TEM (ARA80). Perkin Elmer LS55 and Lambda 45 spectrophotometers were used for PL and UV–Vis measurements, respectively. A WALLIS Zetasizer (Cordouan Tech, France) was used to record the zeta potential (ζ) of the samples. The electrochemical experiments were performed at room temperature in a potentiostat/galvanostat (AUTOLAB PGSTAT302N) with an Ag/AgCl reference electrode, a platinum counter electrode and the prepared Gox|N-GQD|PGE (surface area of 1.76 cm2) as working electrode. In addition, the ZIVE SP1 potentiostat/galvanostat device was used to perform electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) experiments using an AC voltage with an amplitude of 10 mV in the frequency range

Optical properties are crucial for the characterization of N-GQDs to evaluate the quality and band structure [29]. The main techniques used to study as-prepared N-GQDs are UV–Vis absorption and photoluminescence spectroscopy. Fig. 1 (a). shows the UV–vis spectrum of the hydrothermally synthesized N-GQDs. Two distinct absorption bands at 233 and 334 nm are observed, due to the π→π* and n→π* transitions, respectively, originating from C = C, C = O and CN bonds in the N-GQDs [30], which are close to previously reported values [29,31]. In addition, the interaction between Gox and N-GQD|PGE in 0.1 M PBS solution (pH=7.4) was investigated by UV–vis spectroscopy to ensure the integrity of Gox and its active site. As can be seen in Fig. 1(a), the characteristic absorption bands at 233 and 334 nm belong to N-GQD, while an obvious characteristic absorption peak at 279 nm is due to the active site of the enzyme (flavin adenine dinucleotide) [32]. The upper spectrum in Fig. 1(b) (Gox|N-GQD) shows the results obtained from the mixed sample of Gox and N-GQD in the PBS solution to determine the position and shape of the corresponding absorption bands The spectrum showed almost the same peaks as the spectra of Gox and N-GQD, indicating that the preliminary structure of the enzyme was not altered during the immobilization process and its bioactivity was well preserved.

In photoluminescence (PL), an electron is excited to a higher energy level when a molecule absorbs the energy of an incident photon. Then, in a relaxation mood, the electron releases a photon. Based on this, the band gap of GQDs is determined by the operating wavelengths that participate in their PL spectra. Fig. 1(c) shows the PL spectrum for the N-GQD sample. The sample was subjected to nine excitation wavelengths ranging from 300 to 380 nm in order to determine the peak emission intensity. The characteristic emission of GQDs is evident in the spectrum, with the peak at 442 nm (under the stimulation at 340 nm) [33]. The high electron affinity of nitrogen causes a blue shift when it is doped on the GQD lattice. A comparison with results from PL excitation of GQD reported in other studies makes this evident [18]. The spectrum clearly shows that N-GQD is an excitation-independent sample with a minimal degree of defect [29]. Based on the literature reported results in the localization of electron-hole pairs in the isolated sp2 hybridized clusters with dimensionality of ∼ 3 nm yield band gaps consistent with blue emission. Thus, the observed blue PL emissions in N-GQD are mainly due to the size and surface effects [34].

The determination of the N-GQD composition was performed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurement. The full scan spectrum of N-GQD is demonstrated in Fig. 1(d). According to the spectrum, the peaks observed at 284, 401, and 532 eV are attributed to C1s, N1s, and O1s, respectively.The atomic percentages of C: O: N were 61.13: 29.54: 9.33%, and the N/C atomic ratio was obtained at 15.26%. The high-resolution O1s XPS spectrum is presented in Fig. 1(e) can be fitted into two Gaussian peaks at 530.57 eV (C = O) and 531.94 eV (CO). In addition, the high-resolution C1s in Fig. 1(f) can deconvolute into four peaks located at 284.12 eV (CC sp2), 285.20 eV (CN sp2), 287.77 eV (COH), and 289.03 eV (OC = O) [35,36]. The presence of oxidized carbon in the spectrum demonstrate that the formation of the GQD lattice is performed through the formation of citrate salt and the development of the dehydrolysis process between CA molecules in the weak basic environment [31]. The fitting mode of the final signal was evaluated in Fig. 1(g) to find the N configuration in the N-GQDs. Five peaks, representing five relative nitrogen species—pyridinic, pyrrolic, graphitic, oxidized, and chemisorbed nitrogen, respectively—were identified when the N 1 s deconvoluted. These peaks are situated at 399.53, 400.16, 400.94, and 401.80 eV [37,38]. The results suggest that the N atoms have been doped into the graphene lattice [39]. Pyrrolic N represents the nitrogen bonds with carbon in the 5-member-ring structure that are formed from the dehydrolysis process between the neighboring carboxyl and amide groups. The existence of graphite N indicates the N atoms that are bonded with three neighboring C atoms, and pyridine N originates from the N atoms located at the edge of the hexagonal lattice of GQD. The entrance of N atoms in the graphene lattice during the reaction effects on the C sp3. Indeed, N-atom substitution in the GQD structure as n-type domains facilitates free charge carriers’ distribution. The improvement in electron mobilities is due to the enhancement of delocalized electron mobilities without destroying the sp2 hybridized structure in the graphite state [18,40].

Zeta potential was determined for the N-GQD sample in order to examine its electric charge and stability during dispersion. The measured zeta potential of 22.42 mV indicates that N-GQDs have a noticeable negative charge. The dots bind to Gox more readily and immobilize on the SPE when they have a high zeta potential [30]. The values attained are less negative than the ones from the other previous investigations [41–43].

The TEM and FESEM images are shown in Fig. 2 . (a) and (b) represented the monodisperse sheet structure of the N-GQD, with an average particle size of about 10–20 nm. These dots exhibited a uniform shape, indicating a controlled synthesis process. Also, the FESEM image demonstrates the surface of the printed electrode with the sheets of graphene and the point-like structure of the immobilized Gox enzyme on the surface of the graphene layers. The sheets with the multi-pore, large surface-to-volume ratio, and scaly-network surface of PGE can promote the mass transfer of electroactive species and make it an ideal substrate on which a bio-receptor can be stabilized. In addition, the enzyme distribution on the surface is nearly uniform, with a globular structure suggesting immobilization on the electrode. Since the N-GQD facilitates the enzyme immobilization process, the Gox positions on the layers show the sites of the N-GQD on the printed electrodes.

Glucose detection of the nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dot-based screen-printed biosensor involves the electrochemical oxidation of glucose at the electrode surface in present of Gox and N-GQD, followed by the transduction of the generated electrical signal through the printed electrode for glucose detection. The nitrogen doping enhances the sensitivity of the biosensor due to its high electron mobility, allowing for accurate and reliable glucose measurements. The screen-printed format of the biosensor enables mass production and cost-effective manufacturing.

Fig. 3 (a) and (b) present the cyclic voltammetry (CV) of the N-GQD|PGE and Gox|N-GQD|PGE biosensors, respectively, in 0.1 M PBS, pH = 7.4, at a scan rate of 50 mV.s-1 at room temperature in the blank and glucose media. No redox peak was observed at the bare N-GQD|PGE. But a pair of redox peaks were observed at the Gox|N-GQD|PGE in the presence of 0.2 mM glucose. The redox peaks located at potentials of −0.63 V and −1.03 V define the electrocatalytic behavior of the biosensor.

By comparing the CV results of this modified electrode with the unmodified printed electrode using the cross-linking immobilization method described in the previous study [28], it can be seen that the nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots have helped to increase the current density of glucose detection by approximately twice as much. The CV curves of the Gox|N-GQD|PGE biosensor at various glucose concentrations are also displayed in Fig. 3.(c) (0 mM to 20 mM). The biosensor's strong sensitivity to variations in glucose concentration is indicated by the trend of changes in the redox peak's height with rising glucose concentration.

Following the deposition of N-GQDs on PGE and the immobilization of Gox on its surface by Gox-nitrogen linkage, N-GQDs work as a dual-purpose probe, facilitating the enhancement of electron transport while linking to Gox and amplifying its electrochemical activity and electroconductivity. The detection principle of glucose is based on the current change due to the electrochemical reaction on the Gox|N-GQD|PGE after introducing the glucose. In fact, N-GQDs intensify the electrochemical response signals by improving the electron transfer efficiency during their conjugation with glucose.

The direct electron transfer mechanism of the constructed enzymatic biosensor is based on the generation of two electrons from the FAD site of Gox during the redox reaction (Eq. (1)), which are then directly trapped and transferred to the electrode surface by the modifying agents (N-GQDs).
(1) Gox (FDA) + 2e− + 2H+ ⇌ Gox (FDAH2)

Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), a useful technique used for a comprehensive insights of impedance fluctuations at the electrode interface. In a conventional impedance spectrum, the electron transfer-limit process is represented by a semicircle segment at higher frequencies and the diffusion-limit process by a straight line segment at lower frequencies [44]. Here,
Fe
( CN )
6
3 − / 4 −
was employed as a redox probe at the surface of bare SPE and modified N-GQD|SPE. Fig. 4 shows the Nyquist plots of the mentioned electrodes in 5 mM containing 0.1 M KCl as the supporting electrolyte.

The diameter of the semicircle in the high-frequency impedance spectra can be used to determine the charge transfer resistance (Rct), which is crucial for controlling the redox probe's electron transfer kinetics at the surface of the electrode. The diameter of the semicircle for N-GQD|SPE was smaller than that of bare SPE. This evidence represents an enhancement in the electron transfer kinetics by means of N-GQDs settled on the SPE. Indeed, due to the smaller curvature of impedance arcs in the sample, which introduced the beneficial N-GQD on the bare SPE, an increase in charge density and a reduction in the charge transfer barrier were observed, obviously. The results for other modified graphene components that were previously reported are the opposite of this, as they were caused by the presence of charged and polar functional groups presenting a redox pair that penetrated the electrode/solution interference [45].

Moreover, an equivalent electrical circuit (EEC) model was proposed that was based on Randle's circuit. The impedance spectra were fitted by the EC-Lab software to simulate the equivalent circuit fitting for both the bare SPE and N-GQD|SPE As illustrated in the inset of Fig. 4, the EEC is composed of a parallel connection of a charge-transfer resistance Rct and a capacitance Cct, representing faradaic processes. Ion diffusion is taken into account through a finite Warburg impedance Zw connected in series to Rct, and a capacitive element (Cdl) is also added in series.

By fitting the data (inset of Fig. 4), the value of Rct dramatically decreased from bare SPE (49.26 Ω) to N-GQD|SPE (16.35 Ω). The decrease in charge transfer resistance resulting from the incorporation of N-GQDs into the SPE is indicated by the smaller Rct value of N-GQDs|SPE relative to bare SPE. This evidence is consistent with the results of the related article using the N-GQD [46].

Fig. 5 . (a) shows the CV curves of the Gox|N-GQDs|PGE biosensor at different scan rates from 5 mV.s − 1 to 200 mV.s − 1. As the scan rate increased, the current density of redox peaks and peak-to-peak separation also increased. The increase is linearly proportional to the second root of the scan rate ( ν ) with correlation coefficients of R2 = 0.99, which is shown in Fig. 5. (b). Anodic (Ep,a) and cathodic (Ep,c) peaks potentially also shifted to more negative and positive potentials, respectively, by increasing the scan rate. The shift trends show a linear behavior on the logarithm of scan rate (log ν ) with correlation coefficients of 0.99 and 0.97, respectively, for oxidation and reduction peaks (Fig. 5. (c)). Thus, the Gox electrochemical behavior at the Gox|N-GQDs|PGE biosensor represents a quasi-reversible diffusion-controlled redox process near the surface [18,47,48].

The pH of the electrolyte affects the electrochemical behavior of Gox at the Gox|N-GQD|PGE biosensor. Fig. 6 shows the Gox|N-GQD|PGE CV diagrams in electrolytes with different pH values (6.3–13.4), adjusted by HCl and NaOH liquid solutions, at a scan rate of 50 mV.s-1. As can be seen, a pair of redox peaks is obtained for the Gox|N-GQD|PGE biosensor at different pH values. When the pH of the solution increased from 6.3 to 13.4, the current density reached its maximum at pH = 7.4 (inset in Fig. 6). This behavior is due to the pH sensitivity of the enzyme, which affects its electrocatalytic activity. Since Gox has an optimal pH range of around 7, it exhibits the highest activity and efficiency in converting glucose to gluconic acid and hydrogen peroxide at this pH. In acidic or basic pH conditions, the enzyme's activity decreases, leading to a lower current response in the biosensor due to the conformational changes and charge distribution on the enzyme molecule. Therefore, the activity of the enzyme at the pH near human blood (7.4) shows the highest activity level [49].

The integral of CV peaks in thin film voltammetry provides the total charge transferred through the electrode (Q) to reduce or oxidize electroactive enzymes [18], The equation Γ=Q/nFA can be used to determine the active electrode's enzyme surface coverage (Γ). In this equation, n is the number of electrons transferred, F is the Faraday constant, and A is the electrode area (1.76 cm2). The average Γ value from 3 calculations at three different scan rates was 3.33 × 10−7 mol.cm−2, which is much larger than the Gox coverage area on the unmodified PGE (reported in our previous work) that is equal to 1.88 × 10−7 mol.cm−2. The results indicate that a multilayer and three-dimensional Gox coating was formed in the active region of the electrode. The electrode surface area and Gox absorption were further enhanced by the high surface area and strong biocompatibility of N-GQD. Also, the obtained Γ value is higher than the one reported for a GQDs based on carbon ceramic electrode [18], which might be due to the high compatibility of GQDs with graphene sheets of PGE prepared from high-quality graphene ink [28].

Fig. 7 demonstrates the chronoamperometric diagram of the Gox|N-GQD|PGE biosensor at a constant potential of −0.63 V under stirring conditions by injecting 0.4 mM glucose solution into the electrolyte at one-minute intervals. The graph indicates that the electrode's current density varies in response to consecutive glucose injections since it is dependent on variations in catalytic processes and glucose concentration. The response time of the biosensor to flow changes in glucose concentration in the medium was measured to be in the range of 2–5 s. This fast response time can be the result of the high sensitivity of the electrode, which was obtained from the slope of the concentration calibration diagram to be 40.76 mA.mM-1.cm-2. Based on the diagram, the linear range is from the glucose concentration of 1 mM to over 9 mM. The excellent LOD and LOQ were calculated to be equal to 0.098 mM and 0.3 mM, respectively, which are smaller than the unmodified biosensor and the same electrode with the cross-linking immobilizing process previously reported [28].

The Cottrell equation and the graphic in Fig. 8 depict the current for an electrochemical reaction (under mass transport control conditions) of glucose as an electroactive analyte with a diffusion coefficient, D (cm2.s − 1). The diagram of the current density versus t − 1/2 plotted by chronoamperometric data was evaluated for glucose diffusion on the fabricated biosensor.
(2)
I = n F A
D
0.5
C /
π
0.5
t
0.5
where I is the diffusion limiting current, n is the number of electrons, F is the Faraday constant, A is the electroactive area, D is the diffusion coefficient, C is the initial concentration of the analyte, and t is time [50]. From the slope of the linear plot of J vs. t − 1/2, D was estimated at 65.047 cm2.s-1. As this parameter depends on the surface of the diffusing substance, the greater the surface area, the faster the rate of diffusion [51]. The large surface area donated by GQD to printed graphene electrodes enhanced the diffusion rate and effects on the diffusion coefficient, which were larger than in other studies [52,53].

The catalytic reaction rate between the immobilized Gox on the PGE surface and the diffused glucose from the solution is represented by the heterogeneous rate constant (kcat), which is the first-order rate constant [54]. This parameter is evaluated quantitatively by the following equation using the chronoamperometric approach [55].
(3)
I
c a t
/
I L
=
(
π
k
c a t
C g
t
)
1 / 2
where, Icat and IL are the currents in the presence and absence of glucose, respectively, Cg is the glucose concentration, and t is the time.

By plotting Icat/IL versus t1/2 (Fig. 9 ), the value of kcat was calculated from the slope of the line, which is equal to 154.09 mol−1.Sec−1. The velocity constant obtained for this electrode is much higher than that of the Gox-Crosslinked|PGE electrode in previous work [28]. As a result of its structural compatibility, N-GQD can effectively accept and transmit electrons from the enzyme's active region (FAD) quickly, which is responsible for the observed rise in kcat.

glucose detection was evaluated by adding some species that may interfere in blood analysis. Lactose and sucrose as competing interfering species and ascorbic acid, uric acid, and NaCl as non-competing interfering species were investigated for this purpose. Fig. 10 shows a chronoamperometry spectrum for different injections of 0.1 mM glucose and other interfering components during the time. According to the results, the responses of the biosensor to glucose addition are clear, and the stairs appear to be recognizable. However, no obvious stair appears with the injection of other interfering components and has no significant impact on the performance of the glucose sensors. These results indicate the glucose biosensor favored a very high selectivity for glucose in the presence of competing and non-competing analytes.

The electrochemical performance of the Gox|N-GQD|PGE biosensor was studied to evaluate the durability over time. For this purpose, the electrochemical behavior of four electrodes prepared simultaneously was evaluated at 7-day intervals. (The electrodes were kept at 4 °C.) The biosensor response was examined by a glucose oxidation voltammogram (Fig. 11 (a)). As shown in Fig. 11(b), which shows changes in the current density, a negligible decrease in sensor sensitivity was observed after 21 days of biosensor preparation and a decrease of 17.5% compared to the initial signal intensity. To evaluate the sensitivity of the glucose detection signal, an electrochemical calibration curve (current density versus glucose concentration) was plotted, as shown in Fig. 11(c), and the results are summarized in Table 1 . No significant change in sensor sensitivity was observed until day 14. On the 14th day, a decrease in sensitivity was observed from the initial value of 40.760 µA. mM−1.cm−2 to 39.960 µA. mM−1.cm−2, and after 21 days, it reached 39.733 µA. mM−1.cm−2.

Improved catalytic activity in this electrode was achieved by using the N-GQD catalyst due to the very crystalline structure of the nanocatalyst (which caused a fast charge transfer rate), the small particle size (i.e., medium particle size: 4 nm), and the high level of amidation [56]. In fact, this improvement in catalytic activity is probably due to the synergistic effect of N-dopant on the GQD catalyst (resulting in rapid charge transfer) and the enzyme glucose oxidase. According to the obtained results, a comparison of the electrocatalytic performance of Gox-N-GQD|PGE with some graphene-based glucose biosensors is presented in Table 2 .

The N-GQD|PGE was successfully employed to effectively immobilize Gox and serve as a signal amplification platform for the first time. The synergistic effect of N-GQD and the flexible electrode printed from conductive graphene ink in the presented biosensor provided high electrocatalytic activity, excellent conductivity, a large surface area, and size-compatibility with the biomolecule to achieve a high sensitivity of 40.76 mA.mM-1.cm-2 and long-term stability with an LOD of 0.098 mM. The high value of enzyme surface coverage of 3.33 × 10–7 mol.cm-2 also confirmed a superior ability to load the enzyme and a high affinity for biosensor enzyme immobilization. Furthermore, the highly specific and selective properties of such GQD-based electrochemical biosensors originate from their discriminative nature due to the physicochemical characteristics of GQDs. This type of reported GQD glucose biosensor could be a proper candidate for scaling up to mass production.

Naghmeh Gholamalizadeh: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Methodology, Conceptualization. Saeedeh Mazinani: Writing – review & editing, Project administration, Investigation, Funding acquisition. Majid Abdouss: Project administration, Funding acquisition. Ali Mohammad Bazargan: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Fataneh Fatemi: .

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
